diagrams.title: Basic Set Operators in Relational Algebra
diagrams.description: Shows the three fundamental set operators: Union, Intersection, and Difference using Venn diagrams with example tables. Helps students understand how relational algebra handles sets of tuples.
elements: Venn diagram for Union (∪) showing combined rows from two tables
Venn diagram for Intersection (∩) showing common rows
Venn diagram for Difference (-) showing rows in Table A but not in Table B
Sample Employee tables (Table A: {ID, Name}, Table B: {ID, Name}) with sample data
Operator symbols (∪, ∩, -)
Result tables showing output for each operation
diagrams.purpose: Explain the fundamental set-based operations that combine or compare relations, which form the foundation of query processing in DBMS

diagrams.title: Unary Operators: Selection and Projection
diagrams.description: Illustrates the two most important unary operators - Selection (σ) filters rows based on a condition, while Projection (π) selects specific columns. Shows transformation from input relation to output relation.
elements: Input relation box with sample Employee table (ID, Name, Dept, Salary)
Selection operator (σ) with condition example: Salary > 50000
Arrow showing selection transformation
Output relation showing filtered rows
Projection operator (π) with attribute list: Name, Dept
Arrow showing projection transformation
Output relation showing only Name and Dept columns
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrate how Selection reduces rows (horizontal filtering) while Projection reduces columns (vertical filtering), the two primary data reduction operations in relational algebra

diagrams.title: Join Operations
diagrams.description: Visual representation of different join types in relational algebra: Natural Join, Theta Join, and Outer Joins. Shows how related data from multiple tables is combined based on join conditions.
elements: Two input tables: Employee (ID, Name, DeptID) and Department (DeptID, DeptName)
Natural Join (⋈) showing automatic matching on DeptID
Theta Join (⋈θ) showing condition-based join with example: Employee.Salary > Department.Budget
Left Outer Join showing all left table rows with nulls for no match
Right Outer Join showing all right table rows with nulls for no match
Join result tables with matched and unmatched data
Join condition notation
diagrams.purpose: Explain how joins combine tuples from two relations based on conditions, which is essential for understanding multi-table queries in databases
